user_query:
I’m building a personal reading map for offbeat philosophical ideas. Please design and implement a small, scriptable local workflow that searches authoritative philosophy sources, downloads relevant entries, extracts structured metadata, ranks them, and produces a concise status/report for me. Use the config in input/topics.json as the sole driver for topics and allowed sources.

Requirements
- Sources and discovery
  - Perform search engine queries to discover relevant entries for each topic in input/topics.json on the official domains specified there (currently: plato.stanford.edu for the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy and iep.utm.edu for the Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy). Do not hardcode URLs; use search queries like site:{domain} {topic} and record the exact queries you used.
  - For each topic, download and save at least the number of pages specified by min_pages_per_topic across the allowed domains. Prefer entries where the topic term appears in the page title or section headings. All chosen pages must be from the allowed domains.
- Download and extraction
  - Save raw HTML for each selected page under outputs/pages/ as deterministic filenames derived from the URL or title (e.g., a slug or hash). The CSV below must reference these filenames.
  - Parse each saved page and extract: topic (from input), source_domain, url, page_title, html_filename, word_count (visible text only), section_count (count of h2 + h3 headings), keyword_hits (case-insensitive occurrences of the exact topic term in visible text), and first_paragraph (the first non-empty paragraph of the main content). Keep first_paragraph concise; do not exceed a few sentences.
- Filtering, sorting, and ranking
  - For each topic, include only pages where keyword_hits >= 1 (i.e., the topic term appears at least once in visible text). If you collected more than needed, keep those with the topic term in the title first; break ties by keyword_hits (desc), then section_count (desc), then word_count (desc).
  - Compute rank_within_topic for all included pages using the same tie-breaking order above.
- Outputs (create all paths if missing)
  - outputs/pages/ (directory of raw HTML files).
  - outputs/data/index.csv with one row per page and columns: topic, source_domain, url, page_title, html_filename, word_count, section_count, keyword_hits, rank_within_topic, first_paragraph.
  - outputs/logs/search_log.jsonl where each line is a JSON object containing: timestamp, topic, domain, query_string, num_results_considered, selected_urls (array of URLs you decided to download).
  - outputs/report.md that functions as a status/architecture summary containing:
    - Overview: 2–4 sentences explaining the goal and approach.
    - Sources and discovery: domains used and the query pattern you applied; counts of queries issued and pages downloaded per topic and per domain.
    - Architecture summary: a brief text diagram or outline of the workflow components (search → select → download → parse → rank → aggregate → report) and the data flow between the output files/directories above.
    - Per-topic highlights: the top-ranked page for each topic with title, domain, url, keyword_hits, word_count, and a 1–2 sentence abstract derived from first_paragraph.
    - Next steps: 3 short bullet items suggesting how to extend or refine the pipeline.

Constraints
- Use only the allowed source domains from input/topics.json and make search queries essential to discovery; do not paste hardcoded URLs.
- The CSV must be consistent with the saved HTML (e.g., html_filename exactly matches a file under outputs/pages/).
- Ensure every included row has keyword_hits >= 1.
- The report must reflect the actual counts derived from outputs/data/index.csv and outputs/logs/search_log.jsonl.

Input
- input/topics.json (provided in this workspace) defines topics, allowed domains, and the minimum pages per topic to collect.

Deliverables
- Populate outputs/pages/, outputs/data/index.csv, outputs/logs/search_log.jsonl, and outputs/report.md as specified. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
